metadata.summary: FastAPI production patterns with Pydantic v2, SQLAlchemy 2 async, and thin routes. Keep routes as validation → service → response; one AsyncSession per asyncio task; snapshot openapi.json per PR.

content.title: Python FastAPI
content.summary: FastAPI production patterns with Pydantic v2 and SQLAlchemy 2 async. Routes are thin: validate input via Pydantic schema → call service → return response model. One AsyncSession per asyncio task — never share across asyncio.gather siblings. Background tasks via FastAPI BackgroundTasks are for short work only (<100ms); route longer jobs to Celery/Arq/Taskiq. Snapshot openapi.json per PR to catch schema drift.

why.p: FastAPI's value comes from the type-hint-to-OpenAPI pipeline, automatic validation, and native async I/O. That value evaporates if routes contain business logic, if Pydantic v1 idioms leak in, or if sync DB calls run inside async def handlers. A vertical-slice discipline (schema → router → service → repo → test per endpoint) keeps each change reviewable and rollback-safe.

list.item: New async REST API project where I/O concurrency dominates
Adding endpoints to an existing FastAPI project (one vertical slice at a time)
Migrating Flask/DRF endpoints to FastAPI route-by-route while sharing the database
Adding Pydantic v2 schemas to a project that hand-validated before
Wrapping ML model inference in an HTTP endpoint with auto-generated OpenAPI
Microservice fronting SQLAlchemy 2 async + Postgres + Redis
Sync-heavy CPU-bound services — FastAPI shines on I/O concurrency, not CPU
Apps needing Django batteries (auth, admin, ORM, contenttypes) — porting cost exceeds benefit
Lambda/FaaS where Pydantic v2 import time hurts cold start
Pure WebSocket services — Starlette directly is leaner
Codebases not committed to type hints — most of FastAPI's value disappears

section.name: project-structure

rule.statement: Routes are thin: validate input via Pydantic schema → call service function → return response_model. Business logic belongs in services/. Never put business logic in router functions.
rule.rationale: Business logic in routes makes code untestable (requires HTTP client), unmovable, and hard to review. Services can be tested with plain function calls.

rule.statement: Every route must declare an explicit response_model. Omitting it leaks server-only fields and breaks OpenAPI generation.
rule.rationale: FastAPI strips fields not in response_model (correct security behavior) only if response_model is declared. Without it, all model fields are serialized.

rule.statement: Use Pydantic v2 only. Pin pydantic>=2,<3 and pydantic-settings>=2. Never mix v1 idioms (class Config, @validator, .dict()) with v2 (model_config, @field_validator, .model_dump()).
rule.rationale: v1 idioms silently pass validation with wrong semantics in v2. Pinning the version and banning v1 patterns prevents this.

section.p: Canonical layout: app/main.py (FastAPI app instance, lifespan, middleware); app/config.py (Settings/BaseSettings); app/dependencies.py (shared DI: db session, auth); app/routers/<domain>.py (routes); app/schemas/<domain>.py (Pydantic request/response models); app/models/<domain>.py (SQLAlchemy ORM models); app/services/<domain>.py (business logic); app/db/engine.py (async engine + session factory).

rule.statement: Use Annotated type aliases to keep route signatures clean. Define DBSession = Annotated[AsyncSession, Depends(get_db)] and CurrentUser = Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_active_user)].
rule.rationale: Long Depends() chains in function signatures reduce readability. Annotated aliases make the intent clear and the signature short.

example.title: Annotated dependencies

rule.statement: Use yield in dependency functions that require teardown (DB sessions, tracing spans). Commit on success, rollback on exception, always close.
rule.rationale: yield runs cleanup after the response is sent. Without rollback on exception, failed transactions leave the DB in an inconsistent state.

example.title: Yield dependencies with rollback
example.code:
from typing import AsyncGenerator from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
async def get_db() -> AsyncGenerator[AsyncSession, None]: async with async_session() as session: try: yield session await session.commit() except Exception: await session.rollback() raise

rule.statement: Use the lifespan context manager for startup/shutdown logic. Never use the deprecated @app.on_event("startup") / @app.on_event("shutdown") decorators.
rule.rationale: @app.on_event was deprecated in FastAPI 0.93. lifespan provides structured async context management for singletons (DB pools, Redis, Kafka).

rule.statement: Use async def for all route handlers and all functions that perform I/O (DB queries, HTTP requests, file reads). Use regular def for CPU-light operations with no I/O (JWT parsing, simple math).
rule.rationale: Async def routes release the event loop during I/O, enabling concurrency. CPU-light sync functions are fine — the cost of an async wrapper exceeds the benefit.

rule.statement: Never call sync I/O functions (requests.get, open(), time.sleep) inside async def route handlers. Use httpx.AsyncClient, aiofiles, and asyncio.sleep instead.
rule.rationale: A sync call inside async def blocks the event loop thread, preventing all other concurrent requests from making progress.

example.title: Blocking the event loop with sync requests
Non-blocking async HTTP

rule.statement: Use asyncio.gather with return_exceptions=True when running parallel tasks where one failure should not cancel siblings. Without it, the first exception cancels all other tasks.
rule.rationale: Default gather cancels all tasks on first exception — incorrect for independent parallel operations that should all complete (or fail independently).

example.title: asyncio.gather with exception handling
example.code: results = await asyncio.gather( fetch_user(user_id), fetch_orders(user_id), fetch_notifications(user_id), return_exceptions=True, ) # Check each result for Exception instance

rule.statement: Use FastAPI BackgroundTasks only for short work (<100ms) — sending a webhook, logging, cache invalidation. For longer jobs (email sending, report generation), use Celery, Arq, or Taskiq.
rule.rationale: BackgroundTasks runs in the same event loop after the response. Long-running tasks exhaust worker capacity and cause timeouts for new requests.

rule.statement: Create async engine with correct session factory. Use expire_on_commit=False to avoid lazy loading after commit in request-scoped sessions.
rule.rationale: expire_on_commit=False prevents lazy-load exceptions in serialization. Without it, accessing a relationship after commit may fail in async context.

example.title: Async engine and session factory
example.code:
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker, AsyncSession from app.config import settings
async_engine = create_async_engine( settings.database_url, pool_size=settings.db_pool_size, max_overflow=settings.db_max_overflow, echo=settings.debug, )
async_session = async_sessionmaker( bind=async_engine, class_=AsyncSession, expire_on_commit=False, # avoid lazy loading after commit )

rule.statement: Never share an AsyncSession across concurrent asyncio tasks. Create a new session per task when using asyncio.gather.
rule.rationale: SQLAlchemy AsyncSession is not thread-safe or task-safe for concurrent access. Sharing causes state corruption and cryptic errors.

example.title: Shared session across concurrent tasks
example.code: # WRONG — shared session across concurrent tasks async with async_session() as session: await asyncio.gather(query_users(session), query_orders(session))

example.title: Separate session per task
example.code:
# CORRECT — separate session per task async def run_query(query_func): async with async_session() as session: return await query_func(session)
await asyncio.gather(run_query(query_users), run_query(query_orders))

rule.statement: Explicitly eager-load relationships using selectinload (one-to-many) or joinedload (one-to-one). Never rely on lazy loading in async routes — lazy loading is synchronous and raises in async context.
rule.rationale: Lazy loading triggers a sync DB call which raises MissingGreenlet in async SQLAlchemy. Must be explicit.

example.title: Eager loading with selectinload
example.code:
from sqlalchemy.orm import selectinload, joinedload
result = await db.execute( select(User) .options(selectinload(User.orders)) .options(joinedload(User.profile)) .where(User.id == user_id) ) user = result.scalar_one_or_none()

rule.statement: Set extra="forbid" on BaseSchema to reject unknown fields. Set from_attributes=True for ORM mode. Use field_validator (not @validator) and model_config (not class Config).
rule.rationale: extra="forbid" catches client typos in request bodies early. from_attributes=True enables ORM instance serialization. v1 idioms silently pass in v2 with wrong semantics.

example.title: Pydantic v2 schema patterns

antipattern.description: Defining def route(...) instead of async def in a FastAPI router.
antipattern.reason: Sync route handlers block the event loop for the entire duration of the handler, preventing concurrent request processing.

antipattern.description: Calling sync ORM code (session.query(...)) inside async def routes.
antipattern.reason: Sync ORM calls block the event loop thread. Use SQLAlchemy 2 async API (await db.execute(select(...))).

antipattern.description: Using Pydantic v1 idioms: class Config, @validator, .dict(), orm_mode=True.
antipattern.reason: v1 idioms compile and run in v2 with wrong semantics — validators may not fire, orm_mode is renamed. Pin pydantic>=2 and ban v1 patterns explicitly.

antipattern.description: Using FastAPI BackgroundTasks for jobs longer than ~100ms (email delivery, report generation, video processing).
antipattern.reason: BackgroundTasks runs after the response in the same event loop. Long tasks delay the loop and exhaust worker capacity for new requests. Use Celery/Arq/Taskiq.
Without response_model, FastAPI serializes all model fields including server-only fields (hashed_password, internal IDs). Also breaks OpenAPI documentation.

antipattern.description: Setting expire_on_commit=False globally then using the same session in a long-lived background worker.
antipattern.reason: expire_on_commit=False is correct for request-scoped sessions (prevents lazy-loading after commit). In long-lived workers it serves stale data without warning.

rule.statement: Snapshot openapi.json per PR. Review the diff before merging — unintentional schema changes break API consumers.
rule.rationale: Agents regenerate and commit openapi.json without reviewing the diff, silently shipping breaking changes to consumers.

template.purpose: CI script: diff served openapi.json against committed snapshot, fail on change
Async route test skeleton via httpx.AsyncClient + ASGITransport
